name: HashiCorp Boundary (HCP) Access Management API
what_it_does: Open-source privileged access management (PAM) tool with REST API — enabling teams to provide just-in-time, credential-free access to infrastructure (SSH, RDP, databases, Kubernetes) via identity-based access policies, with HashiCorp Cloud Platform managed offering and integration with Vault for dynamic credential injection.
use_cases: Managing privileged access via Boundary API for just-in-time SSH/RDP/database connectivity
Creating targets and host sets via Boundary API for dynamic infrastructure access catalogs
Managing access policies via Boundary API for identity-based zero-trust access control
Integrating Vault credentials via Boundary API for credential injection without exposure
Auditing access sessions via Boundary API for privileged session recording and compliance
not_for: Traditional VPN replacement for bulk network access (Boundary is identity-based, not network)
Simple SSH key management only (use certificate-based SSH for simpler needs)
Non-infrastructure access (Boundary is for infrastructure targets, not SaaS applications)
best_when: A DevOps or security team needs zero-trust access to dynamic infrastructure (Kubernetes, cloud VMs, databases) with credential injection from Vault, without distributing SSH keys or VPN credentials.
avoid_when: You need a traditional PAM product with full session recording, video replay, and enterprise SIEM integration out-of-the-box — evaluate CyberArk or BeyondTrust for mature PAM.

auth.notes: Two modes: (1) Self-hosted Boundary: Bearer token (obtained via boundary authenticate; OIDC, LDAP, password auth methods). (2) HCP Boundary: HCP service principal for management + Boundary token for operations. Token: Authorization: Bearer {boundary_token}. OIDC integration: Okta, Azure AD, GitHub. Token TTL: configurable. Boundary CLI: primary interaction method. Official Go SDK. Vault credential injection: configured in Boundary, not via API call.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open-source self-hosted: free; HCP Boundary: free tier available
pricing.notes: HashiCorp (acquired by IBM 2024 for $6.4B). Open-source self-hosted: Apache 2.0 (pre-BSL; verify current license). HCP Boundary: managed cloud version. Boundary components: Controller (API), Workers (proxy), Desktop client. OIDC integration: integrates with corporate IdP for identity-based access. Session recording: HCP Plus tier. Vault integration: credential brokering for SSH, database, Kubernetes. Targets: dynamic hosts via Vault or static host catalogs. Gateway workers: self-hosted workers for private network access. Used by: security-conscious engineering orgs. IBM acquisition: BSL license implications.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Bearer token from authenticate command. OIDC/LDAP/password auth methods. Workers for private network access. Vault credential injection (not API-configurable at session time). IBM acquisition 2024. BSL license for self-hosted. Go SDK official.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Resource reads idempotent by ID. Target and host catalog creation: use name for idempotency checking. Session establishment: each session is unique. API CRUD operations follow standard REST idempotency.
known_agent_gotchas: Bearer token obtained via 'boundary authenticate' command — short TTL by default (requires re-auth)
Boundary Workers required for private network access — workers bridge HCP controller to internal hosts
Vault credential injection: configured in Boundary target settings (UI/CLI), not injected per-API-call
OIDC integration: Boundary delegates identity to your IdP (Okta/Azure AD) — configure IdP app first
BSL license (2023): self-hosted Boundary changed from Apache 2.0 — verify license for production use
Session recording: only available in HCP Boundary Plus tier — not in free/open-source

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced. Identity-based access: credentials never exposed to users (Vault injection). OIDC integration with corporate IdP for strong identity. Session recording in HCP Plus. Bearer tokens with TTL — short-lived access. FedRAMP authorized. Zero-trust model: no network-level trust. Boundary Workers: control plane in HCP, data plane in your network. IBM acquisition: security posture historically strong. BSL license: open-source security audit more complex post-license change.
